nodes.context: Colombian drug trafficking coalition based in Medellín, active in the late 1970s and 1980s, involved in cocaine production and trafficking.
Colombian drug trafficking organization based in Cali, led by the Rodríguez Orejuela brothers, involved in cocaine trafficking.
Vigilante group formed in 1993 to target Pablo Escobar, financed by the Cali Cartel and led by former Medellín members.
Colombian drug trafficking family that joined the Medellín Cartel, bringing their smuggling networks.
Colombian drug trafficker and enforcer who partnered with Escobar and the Ochoas in the Medellín Cartel.
German-Colombian drug trafficker who partnered with Escobar and Jung, establishing a transshipment operation in the Bahamas.
American drug smuggler who partnered with Lehder and later Escobar to distribute cocaine in the US.

nodes.original_text_name: Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia (Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia or FARC)
nodes.context: Colombian guerrilla group that extorted drug traffickers and kidnapped cartel members, leading to conflict with the Medellín Cartel.
Paramilitary organization formed by Medellín Cartel, Cali Cartel, and others to protect elites from kidnappings.

nodes.standard_name: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
nodes.original_text_name: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
aliases: Association of Middle Magdalena Ranchers and Farmers
nodes.context: Legal front for paramilitary groups, handling logistics and public relations for MAS.
Criminal debt collection service founded by Pablo Escobar in Envigado, Colombia.
Group within the Medellín Cartel that violently opposed extradition to the US.
Colombian guerrilla group that stormed the Supreme Court in 1985.
Mexican drug cartel that cooperated with the Medellín Cartel in the 1980s.
Partial successor to Medellín Cartel, founded as enforcement wing, later affiliated with paramilitaries.
Paramilitary group that affiliated with La Oficina de Envigado for drug trafficking.
Coalition of traffickers opposing extradition, allied with Medellín Cartel (defunct).
Criminal group allied with Medellín Cartel (defunct).
Colombian gang allied with Medellín Cartel (defunct).
Colombian gang allied with Medellín Cartel (defunct).
Spanish criminal groups allied with Medellín Cartel.
Mexican drug cartel allied with Medellín Cartel.

edges.context: Originally had a strategic alliance and smuggling arrangement, sharing distribution hubs in Miami and Houston.
Became enemies after a territorial dispute in 1988, leading to a feud until Medellín's demise in 1993.
Cali Cartel financed Los Pepes, which was formed to target Pablo Escobar.
Los Pepes was formed by former Medellín members and enemies of Pablo Escobar to fight the Medellín Cartel.
The Ochoa brothers joined their smuggling networks with Escobar's to form the Medellín Cartel.
Rodríguez Gacha partnered with Escobar and the Ochoas, contributing his trafficking enterprise and armed men.
Lehder worked with Escobar to establish a cocaine transshipment operation in the Bahamas.
Jung partnered with Escobar after falling out with Lehder, continuing to distribute cocaine for the cartel.
Lehder and Jung were bunkmates in prison and later partnered to smuggle cocaine for Escobar.
The FARC kidnapped cartel members and extorted drug money, leading the cartel to fund private armies against them.
Medellín Cartel members helped found and fund MAS.
Cali Cartel members participated in forming MAS.

edges.target: Asociación Campesina de Ganaderos y Agricultores del Magdalena Medio
edges.context: ACDEGAM served as the legal front and logistics arm for MAS.
Pablo Escobar founded the Oficina de Envigado as a debt collection service for the cartel.
Los Extraditables was a faction within the Medellín Cartel that opposed extradition.
The cartels cooperated in logistics using Nicaragua as a warehouse.
Some claimed the cartel influenced M-19's Palace of Justice siege, but this is disputed.
Los Pepes targeted and assassinated Medellín Cartel members after Escobar's death.
La Oficina de Envigado was founded as an enforcement wing for the Medellín Cartel.
Don Berna fell out with Escobar, causing La Oficina to oust Escobar.
La Oficina de Envigado affiliated with the paramilitary AUC, organizing drug trafficking on their behalf.
Guadalajara Cartel listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
The Extraditables listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
La Corporación listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
Los Priscos listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
Gallón Gang listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
Galician clans listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
Gulf Cartel listed as ally of Medellín Cartel.
Cali Cartel listed as rival of Medellín Cartel.
